title: Learning to adapt through bio-inspired gait strategies for versatile quadruped locomotion
data_sources: Animal locomotion biomechanics literature (dogs, horses, opossums)

data_scope.training_simulator: RaiSim, vectorized, fractal-noise terrain (4 roughness levels)
data_scope.training_compute: Nvidia RTX 3090 GPU; ~6 h (locomotion policy), ~9 h (gait selection policy)
data_scope.gait_correlation_samples: 31 velocity points averaged over 1,000 samples per terrain

core_conclusion: Integrating three core principles of animal locomotion — gait transition strategies, gait procedural memory, and real-time motion adjustments — into a DRL control framework enables a quadruped robot to fluidly switch among eight gaits and recover from instability using only interoceptive sensors. A unified biomechanics reward (CoT, torque saturation, external work, foot-contact error) produces emergent gait transitions that closely match those of dogs and horses. The framework achieves blind zero-shot deployment across 12 real-world terrains, with 18–30% cost-of-transport reduction versus fixed-gait baselines.
why_it_matters: First DRL framework to simultaneously integrate gait memory, dynamic gait selection, and adaptive motion adjustment for quadruped robots; emergent gait transitions match animal data from dogs, horses, and opossums; zero-shot sim-to-real on 12 unseen terrain types using only proprioceptive sensing; auxiliary gaits spontaneously recruited for stability recovery — an untargeted emergent behavior.
why_nature: Novel bio-to-AI abstraction at cognitive rather than mechanical level; unusually broad empirical validation across 12 real terrains and direct animal comparison; cross-disciplinary framing (robots as biomechanics test beds); open code and data on GitHub supporting Nature reproducibility standards.
added_value_to_my_research: IRL from animal locomotion data -> learned distance-decay function for demand-aware R2SFCA from GPS mobility traces
Interoceptive + exteroceptive hybrid architecture -> physics-informed GNN for spatiotemporal prediction with hard physical constraints
Multi-metric unification principle -> PPO + unified reward for EV charging efficiency-equity-fairness optimization
BGS as structured primitive library -> geospatial skill modules for zero-shot transfer of spatiotemporal foundation models to new cities
Domain randomization for zero-shot sim-to-real -> transferable agent-based urban digital twins calibrated once, deployed across unseen cities

related.title: High-speed quadrupedal locomotion by imitation-relaxation reinforcement learning
related.relation: complementary DRL approach to quadruped locomotion via reference-motion imitation, providing contrast to this paper's bio-inspired gait scheduler approach

related.title: Multi-Modal Mobility Morphobot (M4) with appendage repurposing for locomotion plasticity enhancement
related.relation: nature-inspired multi-modal locomotion with terrain-adaptive strategy, complementing the gait diversity and stability-recovery themes

related.title: Efficient and scalable reinforcement learning for large-scale network control
related.relation: scalable DRL with PPO for complex sequential decision-making, a methodological parallel to the gait selection policy training

related.title: Reinforcement learning in artificial and biological systems
related.relation: foundational review of bidirectional concept flow between biological and artificial RL systems — directly relevant to this paper's bio-to-robot abstraction

related.title: Model-based reinforcement learning for ultrasound-driven autonomous microrobots
related.relation: model-based RL for robot control in physical environments, relevant to sim-to-real transfer methodology comparison

figures.caption: Framework architecture: gait transition strategies in the gait selection policy (pi_G), gait procedural memory in the bio-inspired gait scheduler (BGS), and adaptive motion adjustments in the locomotion policy (pi_L).
Zero-shot deployment snapshots across 4 simulated terrain roughness levels and 12 real-world terrain types including wood-chip, rocks, loose timber, and low-friction ramp.

Locomotion comparison study: bio-inspired policy vs. baselines (no gait references, no state estimator) on velocity tracking, contact schedule tracking, and stability across flat to very rough terrain.
Individual gait vs. unified gait selection policy (pi_G^uni) on CoT, torque saturation, external work, and foot-contact error; radar plots show relative performance across terrain levels.
Quantitative comparison of robot gait selection strategies with animal data (dogs, horses, opossums) during increasing forward velocity; metric correlation maps for flat and rough terrain.
Real-world stability recovery events on loose timber, muddy grass, and low-friction board; metric heatmaps preceding gait transitions; comparison with animal auxiliary gait use.
